title: Angular Error Handling – Building Resilient Apps

meta.title: Angular Error Handling Guide – Global ErrorHandler & CatchError (2026)
meta.description: Learn professional error handling in Angular. Master global ErrorHandlers, RxJS catchError for APIs, and user-friendly error boundaries.

content.value: In an enterprise environment, an unhandled exception can break the entire user experience. As a Technical Lead, your goal is to ensure that when things go wrong—whether it's a 500 Server Error from your Python backend or a runtime null pointer—the application fails gracefully. Angular provides multiple layers of defense, from local RxJS operators to a global 'Catch-All' mechanism.

Since Angular's `HttpClient` returns Observables, we use the `catchError` operator to intercept failed network requests. This allows you to log the specific error and return a fallback value or a user-friendly message to the component.

Angular provides a default `ErrorHandler` class that logs errors to the browser console. For professional apps like Revochamp, you should override this to send errors to a logging service (like Sentry) or show a global 'Something went wrong' UI notification.
Instead of adding `catchError` to every single service, you can use a functional interceptor to handle common HTTP status codes (like 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden) in one place.

faq.question: Should I use try-catch with HttpClient?
faq.answer: No. HttpClient is asynchronous and uses Observables. A try-catch block will not catch errors from an Observable stream. You must use the 'catchError' operator within the '.pipe()' method.

faq.question: How do I show an error message to the user?
faq.answer: The best practice is to have the service 'throw' the error. The component then subscribes to the service and uses the 'error' callback of the '.subscribe()' method to set a local 'errorMessage' variable displayed in the HTML template.

faq.question: What is HttpErrorResponse?
faq.answer: It is a specialized class in Angular that provides detailed information about a failed HTTP request, including the status code (e.g., 404), the status text, and the error body returned by the server.
